name: Image as Witness: Portfolio Review with Palani Kumar
description:
Image credit: M. Palani Kumar
A portfolio review session led by photographer and journalist M. Palani Kumar, founder of the People’s Photographers Archive, whose work documents the lives of working-class women and marginalized communities. The session offers an opportunity to explore how personal stories can be situated within broader societal narratives through visual practice, while engaging with the idea of “witness” in long-term documentary work. Drawing on projects spanning 8–9 years, it reflects on themes of caste violence, forced evictions, and environmental degradation, examining their impact on both ecology and the communities that depend on it. 

Participants are invited to present portfolios that engage with caste- and lineage-based narratives as lived experiences, and to consider how to document working-class lives, gender, and labour with sensitivity and accountability. Emphasizing ethical representation and storytelling from within communities, the session foregrounds the importance of centering and amplifying voices that are often marginalized or unheard. 

Please submit your portfolio on the form by May 10, 2026 9pm IST. 

This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People's Archives of Rural India (PARI)

name: Living Botanicals
description:
Join us for a guided walkthrough of the Living Botanicals installation in the MAP’s Sculpture courtyard led by its grower Kush Sethi. Kush is a gardener and a forager from Delhi who has helped us create a living and breathing space, in collaboration with Architect Bhavana La Noche. In May, he returns to engage our audiences with gardening and walkthroughs of the plants in this installation.

Come along to hear tales behind this garden’s inception, behind-the-scenes and stories of plants from Hortus Malabaricus to Bangalore. Spend time in this living ecosystem observing plants from the lens of an explorer. We have also provided a field kit of assorted lenses which will allow you to zoom into the ecology of this garden. Bring a diary to draw, press things and take notes.

name: Summer at MAP (Ages 8-12)
description:
Spend the summer inside a museum, learning from professional artists. Our workshops for children aged 8–12 are hands-on, creative, and fun, covering everything from tie-dye to ceramics and more. 

Choose your experience

Register for individual workshops, or go all-in with our combination ticket for access to every session throughout the camp.

May 4

Ceramics and Mask Making Workshop

May 5

Anthotype and Cyanotype Workshop

May 6

Canvas City

May 7

Growing From Nothing: A Gardening Walk Workshop

May 8

Natural Tie-Dye Workshop

End-of-camp showcase

The final day is a celebration where the participants present everything they made over the summer, open for all families to attend.

Note: 

-Please pack a lunch, snacks, and a water bottle for your child each day.

-Every registered participant receives a gift store voucher of equal value, redeemable at the MAP Gift Store. 

-If you're a working parent, our café space is open for you to use while your child is in session.

These workshops are for children between the ages of 8-12. If you are between the ages of 13-16 check out our programme here.

Spots are limited. Register early to secure your place.

name: Climate Change and its Impact on Women’s Mental Health
description:
This talk looks at how changing weather, excessive heat, floods and more are causing mental distress to agricultural workers. Older women, already burdened by age-related health issues, are finding it harder  to cope. The burden on women is disproportionately higher—fetching firewood and water takes more time and energy as summers arrive earlier, last longer and are hotter than before. Cooking on burning fires adds to their overall heat stress, often a job they do outside the home as well.

This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People’s Archives of Rural India (PARI)

name: Summer at MAP (Ages 13-16)
description:
Five days, a museum full of inspiration, and a workshop unlike any other, join film maker Hansa Thapliyal for doll-making and stop-motion animation at MAP.

Using the museum as a creative playground, participants will work with everyday materials and scraps to build their own dolls, drawing inspiration from our exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky. Along the way, they'll explore their ideas, experiment with storytelling, and find their own creative voice.

Through playful stop-motion exercises, these ideas will begin to move, building into a collaborative animated film screened at the end of Summer at MAP.

Participants are required to bring:

-A mobile phone with a camera

-A few discarded items from home to add a personal touch to the creations

Note: 

-Please pack a lunch, snacks, and a water bottle for your child each day.

-Every registered participant receives a gift store voucher of equal value, redeemable at the MAP Gift Store. 

-If you're a working parent, our café space is open for you to use while your child is in session.

These workshops are for children between the ages of 13-16 If you are between the ages of 8-12 check out our programme here.

Spots are limited. Register early to secure your place.

name: Photo Talk: Land, Livelihood, and Loss
description:
Join us for a photo talk that journeys through the lives of nomadic pastoral communities whose movement, labour, and knowledge have long shaped the ecologies of the Indian subcontinent – across mountains, deserts, forests and more. Presented as part of the public programming for Beneath the Turning Sky, and in collaboration with People’s Archive of Rural India (PARI), this session reflects on pastoralism as both livelihood and way of life—deeply attuned to seasons, landscapes, and the needs of livestock. .

Through photographs and conversation, the talk will trace the lives of the Changpas of Ladakh and the Fakirani Jats of Kachchh—communities navigating fragile ecosystems while facing mounting pressures from climate change, industrial expansion, and displacement. Bringing together image-making and oral histories, the session invites audiences to consider migration, resilience, and the futures of communities whose histories are often overlooked. 

This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People’s Archives of Rural India (PARI)

name: The Ghosts of Conservation - Political Ecology of High Altitude Himalayan Landscapes
description:
Image Credit: Rustam Vania

At the top of the high-altitude Himalayan ranges lives the snow leopard, elusive and solitary, known as the ghost of the mountains. To protect it is to protect the fragile world it moves through. 

This illustrated talk and interactive workshop takes you into that world, tracing the delicate balance between human livelihoods, conservation, and the restoration of Himalayan ecosystems.

 

Please note the interactive workshop has a few slots and requires registration. The illustrated talk, open for all, is from 7:00 PM to 8:30 PM.

name: Songs of Labour: Living Archives
description:
Join us for a special listening session around the Grindmill Songs Projectand Songs of the Rann, by the People's Archive of Rural India (PARI), which brings together sound, memory, and conversation. Facilitated by Namita Waikar and Pratishtha, this session explores songs as intimate emotional archives—voices that carry lived histories of women’s labour, resilience, longing, humour, and dissent. 

Sung while performing everyday work, these songs reveal how labour, caste, gender, and patriarchy are woven into daily life, while also creating spaces of expression and solidarity. Through collective listening and discussion, we will reflect on the power of oral traditions and informal cultural transmission in preserving stories and sustaining memory across generations.



This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People’s Archives of Rural India (PARI)

name: Ethics of Documenting Labour
description:
What constitutes labour — and who gets to define it? Labour takes many forms, yet documentation has long been shaped by those with the privilege to record, which means it has also long been shaped by what they choose to overlook. The everyday work of women in the home, the intricate skill behind craft processes, the invisible hands in agriculture, these rarely make it into the frame.
How do we gather information that doesn't default to the most visible, the most legible, the most convenient?
Caste is perhaps the sharpest example of this blind spot. The labour it assigns is among the most physically demanding and socially stigmatising, and yet it remains among the most systematically erased from memory, archive, and public acknowledgement.
To truly account for labour is to reckon with who has been seen, and who has been made invisible, and by whom.
This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People’s Archives of Rural India (PARI)

name: Learning Lounge
description:
A series of faculty-led sessions in the form of talks, lectures or workshops are delivered by faculty members of Srishti Manipal Institute of Art, Design & Technology. These sessions range from sounds, urban biodiversity, metaphor-making, livelihoods, ecosystems and more.

Schedule
April 7
On Seeing the Sound in a Changing Environment by Dr Guruaja, K. V.

April 18
Where Have all the Sparrows Gone? by Aparna Raman

April 21
Slow Disturbances by Priya Joseph

April 25
Climate Metaphors by Asmita Sarkar

May 5
The Ghosts of Conservation by Rustam Vania

May 9
Climate Canvases by Dr Chandrima Home and Dr Sambita Modak

Please note: Each session requires individual registration. Some sessions require you to bring certain materials so please be prepared.

name: Changing Landscapes: Films on Climate, Gender & Labour
description:
Join us for a screening of documentary films by People's Archive of Rural India (PARI) that bring into focus the intertwined realities of climate change, gender, and labour across rural India. Through intimate and powerful storytelling, these films trace how shifting ecologies reshape livelihoods, deepen existing inequalities, and place disproportionate burdens on women and marginalised communities. 

From farms and forests to coastlines and homes, the screenings reveal stories of resilience, adaptation, and collective action in the face of environmental change. This programme invites audiences to reflect on climate justice not as an abstract future concern, but as a lived present shaped by work, care, and survival.



This event is part of programming around Beneath the Turning Sky in collaboration with People’s Archives of Rural India (PARI)

workPresented.name: Changing Landscapes: Films on Climate, Gender & Labour

name: Plant Parenting 101
description:
A session that moves from gallery to garden.
Begin with a guided walkthrough of Paper Gardens , before stepping into the Living Botanical installation with its gardener, Kush Sethi. Drawing connections between the artworks and the plants around you, Kush will guide you through observing species, forms, compositions, and the quiet details of plant anatomy.
The session then transitions into a hands-on gardening workshop, slow-paced and unhurried, where you will practise the art of potting and composing. A gentle introduction to becoming a plant parent.
Your completed piece is yours to take home.

name: Holding Space: Feminist Reading Circle
description:
What does vulnerability look like? What does care feel like?
These are the questions that artist Afra Eisma leaves us with in warrior garments.

Join us for a three-part series of reflective feminist reading sessions, developed in collaboration with Zubaan and  facilitated by Laxmi Murthy. Together, we will explore gender-based violence—its lived realities, and the possibilities of resistance, solidarity, and healing. Through stories, essays, and personal reflections, you are invited to engage with the exhibition at your own pace, in a space that encourages listening, sharing, and care.

Note:

-This is the first session in a three-part series presented alongside Warrior Garments. Each session is self-contained, but we encourage participants to attend all three.

-The upcoming sessions will take place on May 30 and June 21, 2026.

-Reading materials for each will be shared after registration.

Trigger Warning: The sessions may include discussions on sexual violence.

name: Climate Canvases
description:
Image Credit: Dr Sambita Modak

Through this workshop, we will connect the audience to the impacts of climate change on ecosystems and how these impacts affect the series of socio-economic linkages around us. The session will focus on three key lenses: biodiversity, food and water systems, and social fabric, connecting these through storytelling and data visualisation. 

Through art explorations, we will examine themes such as climate grief, climate injustice, and the interconnection between them and ecosystems.
